blocks.content: Preparations always take longer than planned. The goal is not to have a beautifully furnished van right away, but to ensure the basics function.

images.alt: The solar charge controller and the inverter
The bed
A stable internet connection for online work

blocks.content: There is a bed to sleep on, the electricity works and there is an internet connection, so we can leave for a test drive. But then comes an unexpected turn of events: the van is not yet fully mechanically sound, so we cannot leave yet! When traveling in a van, you always have to be able to handle unexpected twists. Adjusting plans, and above all realizing that this is not so bad at all, and that traveling is largely about this ability to adapt. The advantage now is that I can start doing that even before a test drive has begun!

The van was checked mechanically and we are ready to hit the road! There might be a few extra things that need to be done later, but for now, we are on our way. So, really picking up the van next week! And I promise I will post more photos then too.

It was actually an advantage to wait another week, because now some extra things could be done. The solar panels have been permanently attached, the wardrobe has been installed, and the gas stove has also passed its first test. Tomorrow a few final preparations, and then off for some test drives!

images.alt: The toilet
When the toilet is closed, it's just a normal seat
The second seat, aka the closet
Stove
Solar panels

blocks.content: The long drive to Belgium went well, and then a short trip to a beautiful lake in the area. Tomorrow we leave the solid soil behind and embark on the real journey.

The first steps to get on the way were to pack all my stuff, store it in the van and clean the apartment. This was pretty intense, but with great help it all worked out very well! (Thank you W&J!!) Then most of my stuff was offloaded to give away, and some to store, and then this picture shows what's left. Before it nearly reached the ceiling of the van! You always have more stuff than you thought, even after you scaled down already in preparation to move into the van.

images.alt: Even after getting rid of most stuff, inside it's still a mess (with also some nice things! ;)

Instead of the picture at the top of this post, I might also have shown this one. A bit less messy, I cleaned up just to take this picture. But what's the best to show on a blog, the messy reality or sometimes a slightly cleaner version? (What do you think? Let me know in the comments below! Oh no...:D

blocks.content: Then the real journey starts. The electricity suddenly didn't work the first day, but that turned out to be just a low battery. The water pump keeps running, so I just disconnect it when not needed. The teething troubles of a 'new' van. But we're on the road, first visiting the Dutch National Education Museum in Dordrecht, and then the nearby Nature reserve De Biesbosch.

images.alt: Education museum
Biesbosch 1
Biesbosch 2
Biesbosch 3
Biesbosch 4
Biesbosch 5

blocks.content: It has already been a week, so it is time for a new post. Last week I was still in the Netherlands, where the journey took me past the tulip fields and included a visit to my first student city, Leiden. Then followed the first really big trip to Switzerland, where I continued the journey alone as of yesterday. Traveling alone or with someone is a big difference; now the real challenge begins of being 'on the road', working, eating, relaxing, and reflecting while traveling.

images.alt: The place where it all started nearly 20 years ago
The flower fields next to Keukenhof
On the road in Germany. Thanks to a lovely photographer for this beautiful picture.

blocks.content: Besides work starting again, there is also time for relaxation: a walk to one of the peaks in the Jura Mountains in France, with a view over the Geneva Lake and the Mont Blanc. Fortunately, the ski season is already over, so it is quiet here. For those following this blog for the beautiful photos like the ones below: it will be a little while before I come across such spectacular views again. Starting tomorrow, I will slowly travel further north again, to pass by Belgium and the Netherlands next week.

images.alt: An early guest at the parking place this morning
The empty ski slope
The ski slope from above
The melting snow on the hiking trail
The road appears again
Summit
Finding back my footsteps on the way back
Where is the path?
Back home. For the exact route of this walk, see my Komoot account.

blocks.content: This time, a slightly more extensive update. On the way back to Leuven and the Netherlands, there are two places I wanted to visit, and the first of these is the canal pictured above. This canal is special for several reasons. It connects the Seine with the Saône and Rhône, making it possible for the first time to reach the Mediterranean Sea from Paris via France's inland waterways. The construction of the canal is impressive, as a large difference in elevation had to be overcome. This resulted in 189 locks and, at the highest point, a tunnel more than 3 km long. But for me personally, this canal is also special because I stumbled upon it by chance during my cycling trip through Europe in the summer of 2013. After conquering many mountains and hills, it was a relief to be able to follow a cycling path along the water. Almost thirteen years ago, at that time already I thought: when I am a bit older, and no longer make such a trip with a bike and a tent, I will do it in a van. And now I am here, cycling on my mountain bike along those same bike paths as almost 13 years ago. What a different journey, and what a different world. Back then, I didn't have a smartphone yet, and in the beginning, I even set off without a map. I sent a text message once a week to let my family know where I was. Now I use my smartphone constantly: to look up where I can park the camper for the night, for navigation, I look up where I can refuel or do groceries, and I search for hiking and cycling trails via Komoot. Moreover, I work online and maintain this blog. How different this journey is now compared to 13 years ago, but also: how different the world is now. The contrast with 13 years ago makes me reflect on that as well. I am happy with all the online options that make this current journey possible, and at the same time, when the news comes along or when I see titles on YouTube screaming for attention, the world of 2026 feels so much more chaotic than the world of 2013. Part of this current journey is also a detachment from a frenzied existence, and at the same time, I do not want it to be a flight, a withdrawal from responsibility for this world. Where are all these considerations leading? Hopefully, that will become clearer over the next months.

images.alt: The van on the left, next to the water basin at the highest point of the canal
Further along the canal becomes narrower
To supply the canal at its highest point of enough water, this basin was constructed
At the village Pouilly-en-Auxois the canal disappears underground
Even though it is narrower, it still is an impressive waterway, that was excavated in the rock over a length of 3333 meter with handdrills and black powder (in the years 1826-1832)
Keeping a good hold of my phone while taking this picture!
To construct the tunnel, shafts were built that needed to reach up to 40 meters deep to reach the canal level
Altitude of the shaft above sea level
From 1892 onwards an electric tugboat was used to pull ships through the tunnel
The houses along the canal that were in the past inhabited by the lockkeepers are also interesting
Some have a more modern attachment built to them
Do the children really play this close to the water?
Some houses are abandoned

blocks.content:
The second destination I absolutely wanted to visit was Verdun. Already when I cycled through northern France 13 years ago, I realized that it would be much easier to visit the various historical sites connected to the First World War with a van. And where better to start than in Verdun? 
The first destination was the citadel, an underground stronghold from which the city's defense was coordinated. A modern tour through the citadel's underground tunnels consists of a ride in a self-driving vehicle for a maximum of 5 people, where you put on VR glasses to see various scenes from that time in the corridors. However, the result remains somewhat disappointing. Typically enough, the story told during the tour is about 4 soldiers coming from the front line to attend a ceremony. The underground citadel is safe, where the soldiers' raw experiences cannot find a place. All of this comes a little closer when you arrive in Fleury-devant-Douamont. This small village was right on the front line, and was captured by the Germans multiple times and recaptured by the French. It was completely destroyed and never rebuilt, but the streets and houses of that time have been made visible again by street signs and stones marking the houses, shops, and other buildings. All of this is situated in a landscape where the bomb craters are still visible. Even as you drive further through these woods, that is the scene: a crater landscape, reclaimed by nature, yet still clearly visible. Perhaps that speaks even more than all the memorial stones for peace and brotherhood erected here and there, the raw power of the shells, the wounds in the earth, still so clearly visible after exactly 110 years. 
And then you drive further, out of the forest, and you reach the first inhabited vilage again. Here people live, in warmth and shelter. Precious and fragile.

images.alt: Trees in bomb craters
The village
The War Cemetery with 16.000 graves
War Cemetery with view on the Ossuary
In the Ossuary the remains of 130.000 not identified soldiers are laid to rest
The entrance of the Citadel
Monument in the city of Verdun

blocks.content: Besides all these heavy themes, van life continues in its everyday aspects. Cooked a real meal for the first time, cooked rice for the first time using energy from the solar panels. And some final images from the Jura Mountains, with two beautiful lakes, and the first short mountain bike ride I took there.

images.alt: An oldfashioned meal
